instruction: Explain LL vs LR parsing. When would I choose each for building a parser?
output:
<think>
LL parsers (top-down, left-to-right, leftmost derivation): simpler to implement, especially LL(1) which can be hand-written as a recursive descent parser. Limited in the grammars they can handle (no left recursion).

LR parsers (bottom-up, left-to-right, rightmost derivation): handle a larger class of grammars, including left-recursive ones. More complex to implement (usually generated by tools like yacc/bison). LALR(1) is the practical variant used by most parser generators.

Choose LL for: simple languages, hand-written parsers, error messages matter. Choose LR for: complex grammars, tool-generated parsers, maximum grammar coverage.

instruction: Explain type inference and how it works in languages like TypeScript and Rust.
output:
<think>
Type inference determines types without explicit annotations by analyzing how values are used. The Hindley-Milner algorithm (used in ML, Haskell) works by: (1) assign type variables to all expressions, (2) generate constraints from usage (if x + 1, then x must be numeric), (3) solve constraints by unification.

TypeScript uses local type inference (flow-based): infer from assignments and control flow. Rust uses similar local inference with lifetime inference added. Both avoid the need to annotate every variable while still providing full type safety.

instruction: What is SSA form and why do compilers use it for optimization?
output:
<think>
Static Single Assignment: each variable is assigned exactly once. Instead of x = 1; x = x + 1, SSA uses x1 = 1; x2 = x1 + 1. At control flow joins, phi functions merge values: x3 = phi(x1, x2).

Benefits: (1) constant propagation is trivial (each definition is unique), (2) dead code elimination is easy (unused variables have no uses), (3) register allocation is simpler (variable lifetimes dont overlap). Used by LLVM, GCC, V8 (JavaScript), and most modern compilers.

instruction: Explain JIT compilation and when it outperforms ahead-of-time compilation.
output:
<think>
JIT (Just-In-Time) compiles code at runtime, after observing actual execution patterns. Advantages over AOT: (1) can specialize for actual types (if a function always receives integers, generate integer-optimized machine code), (2) can inline virtual method calls after observing the most common target, (3) can optimize hot paths based on profiling data.

Disadvantages: startup latency (compilation happens at runtime), memory overhead (compiler in memory), warmup period (code is slow until JIT kicks in). Used by: JVM (HotSpot), V8 (JavaScript), PyPy (Python), .NET.
